conversation.message: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.

You've been smoking bidis for an incredibly long time, and it's amazing that you've managed to reduce your daily intake a bit because of your doctor's advice. 

What made you decide to listen to your doctor and try to cut down on your bidis consumption in the first place?
The doctor kept telling me about my breathing problems and said it would get worse. I get a lot of coughing, especially in the cold weather. I'm worried about my health.

You're worried about your health, and it sounds like the doctor's concerns have really resonated with you. What specific aspect of your health are you most worried about changing or improving if you reduce your bidis consumption?
The coughing. It's hard to work in the fields when I'm coughing all the time. It hurts and it's hard to breathe.

It sounds like the coughing is really impacting your daily life, especially when you're working in the fields. Can you tell me more about what you imagine your life would be like without the coughing?
Just being able to work without getting tired so fast. And being able to breathe properly. That would be a big change.

You're envisioning a life where you have more energy and can breathe easily, which is a wonderful goal to work towards. What do you think would be the first step for you to start feeling more like that today?
I think I should just try to reduce my smoking some more. From 3 or 4 bidis a day, maybe I can try to go to 2 or 1.

It sounds like you're feeling motivated to take small steps towards reducing your bidi consumption, which is a great place to start. What do you think would make it easier for you to stick to reducing to 2 or 1 bidis a day, rather than going back to 3 or 4?
If I can find something else to do in the morning when I wake up, like drinking tea or going for a walk. Maybe that would help me not think about smoking as much.

You're thinking of trying to replace the morning smoking habit with a new activity, like drinking tea or going for a walk. What do you think you would need to make sure you follow through with this new morning routine?
I need to remind myself to do it every day. Maybe I can tell my wife to remind me too, if she sees me reaching for a bidi.

You're acknowledging the importance of reminders to help you stay on track with your new morning routine. How do you think you'll feel if you miss a day or two of following through with your new routine?
I'll probably feel frustrated. And maybe I'll want to smoke again.

It sounds like you're worried that missing a day or two of your new morning routine will make you feel frustrated and want to smoke again. What do you think is the most important thing for you to remember when you're feeling frustrated about missing a day?
That one day doesn't matter. I can start again the next day. And the doctor said it's a long process, so I should be patient.

It sounds like you're feeling more confident about your ability to stick to your new morning routine and that you're reminding yourself that it's okay to have setbacks. What do you think would happen if you were able to reduce your bidi usage by just one or two a day for the next week?
I think I might start feeling a little better. My breathing might not be as bad. And I might have more energy.

You're mentioning the potential benefits of reducing your bidi usage, such as feeling better, improved breathing, and increased energy. Can you think of any ways to celebrate these small victories to help motivate you to keep moving forward?
Maybe I can tell my wife about it. She's always telling me I'm doing a good job with my health, so maybe I can show her I'm making progress.
It sounds like you're thinking of sharing your progress with your wife as a way to celebrate and get support. Can you imagine how you think she would react when you share this with her?
She'll be happy. And she might even help me remember to keep up the good work.

The patient is saying that they think their wife will be happy and supportive when they share their progress with her. 

What did you plan to do to help your wife remember to remind you to keep up the good work, now that you've mentioned that as something she might do?
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
You've been smoking bidis for a pretty long time. How long do you think you'd like to stop smoking for, just to start with?
I don't know, a month maybe? Or at least till a big project I'm working on finishes. I've got to earn some money.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain and also have a practical reason to want to quit smoking. What do you think might happen if you were to use that big project deadline as a goal to stay smoke-free?
I'd save some money, I guess. And maybe my wife wouldn't yell at me so much about spending too much on bidis. But what if I fail? I'll just feel bad, and the guys on site will still be smoking.

It sounds like you're worried about feeling bad if you fail to quit smoking during this project deadline, and also about not being able to fit in with your coworkers. What's one small step you think you could take to prepare yourself for quitting, even if it's just a tiny change?
I suppose I could try to limit myself to just one packet a day while I'm working on this project. That way, even if I don't quit completely, at least I'll be saving some money.
That sounds like a good first step, limiting yourself to one packet a day. What kind of situations do you think might make it really hard for you to stick to just one packet a day?
My coworkers are always offering me a bidi, saying 'have one, yaar'. When we're on break or after lunch. If they're smoking, it's hard for me to say no.

It sounds like your coworkers' offers are making it really tough for you to say no to a bidi, especially when they're smoking. How will you remind yourself of your goal to limit yourself to one packet a day when they're asking you to join them?
I don't know, maybe just say "no thanks, I'm cutting down" or something? I don't want to lie to them, but I also don't want to give in to the bidi.

It sounds like you're feeling torn between being honest with your coworkers and staying on track with your goal. Can you practice saying "no thanks, I'm cutting down" out loud with me right now, so you can feel more confident in your ability to respond to their offers?
"No thanks, I'm cutting down." (pausing) Yeah, that sounds okay, I guess.

You seem to be feeling a bit more confident about saying "no thanks, I'm cutting down" to your coworkers. Now that you've practiced, do you think you can imagine a scenario where you might say those exact words to your coworkers, and what would you expect your reaction to be?
I think I could say it to them, and they'd probably just ask me what's wrong. They might even make fun of me a bit. I don't know how they'd really react, but I'm not sure I want to find out.

You're feeling uncertain about how your coworkers might react if you say no to a bidi, and it's making you hesitant to try it out. Do you think their reaction would be more about you quitting or more about them not understanding why you're quitting?
I think they'd be more annoyed that I'm quitting, not understanding why I'm doing it. They'd just think I'm being crazy or weak.

You're feeling concerned that your coworkers will think you're weak or crazy for quitting, and that's making you hesitant to try saying "no thanks, I'm cutting down." Can you think of a time when you saw someone you respect making a tough choice, and what impact that had on you?

My uncle, my dad's brother. He lost a lot of weight a few years back. I think he was around 60 at the time. He was always a heavy eater, but he just decided to cut back on food and start exercising. I remember thinking he was crazy at first, but then I saw how much better he felt, and I was impressed. He's still doing it now, and he looks really good.
You're telling me about your uncle's transformation, and how seeing him make a positive change really inspired you. What was it specifically about your uncle's journey that impressed you the most?

I think it was just how normal he was. He didn't pretend to be someone he wasn't, and he didn't make a big show of it. He just did it, and it made a big difference. That's what I want to be like, I guess. Just making a change, bit by bit.

You're saying that your uncle's approach to making a change inspired you because he seemed so normal and genuine in his efforts. How do you think you can apply that same sense of authenticity to your own journey of quitting bidis?
I think I just need to be okay with being a little weird, you know? Not try to be someone I'm not, just be honest with myself and my coworkers about what I'm trying to do.

You're recognizing that being true to yourself and being open about your struggles is a key part of this journey. Can you think of one specific situation coming up at work where you might feel tempted to hide your efforts to quit bidis?
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.
It sounds like you've been smoking bidis for a long time and have started to cut back a bit, which is a great step. What motivated you to try to reduce your smoking in the first place?
My daughter, she brought me here. She's concerned about my health, says I get out of breath easily when it's cold outside. And I guess she wants me to be healthier.
It sounds like your daughter is really concerned about your health and wants to see you do well, which is wonderful. What does it mean to you to be a healthier father for your daughter?

It means... I want to live long enough to see her grow up, married with kids of her own. My wife and I always talked about that. Now it's just me, and I don't want to leave her alone without a father.

It sounds like being there for your daughter's milestones is really important to you, and you're scared about not being able to experience those moments with her. What does it feel like when you think about the possibility of not being there for her?
It feels... empty. Like a part of me is already gone. My wife, she was the one who always made me feel needed. Now, without her, I just feel... lost.
It sounds like you're feeling a deep sense of loss and emptiness, especially without your wife. Can you tell me more about what it was like when your wife was alive, and how she made you feel needed?

(pausing, looking down) She was always there for me, supported me in everything. We'd sit together in the evenings, and she'd listen to me talk about my day. She had a way of making me feel like I was the only person in the world. I don't feel that way anymore, since she's... gone. (pausing again, collecting thoughts) I'm just trying to make it through each day, to be honest.

It sounds like you're really missing your wife's presence in your life, and it's making it hard for you to feel like yourself again. Can you think of a situation recently where you felt a strong urge to smoke, and how you handled it?

(thoughtfully) Yeah... I think it was the other night, around 3 AM. I woke up, couldn't sleep, and I just started thinking about my wife. I felt so alone, and I picked up a bidi without even realizing it. But I was able to put it down, after a while. My daughter's been reminding me to take care of myself, so I guess that helped.

It sounds like those late-night moments are really tough for you, and it's great that you were able to put down the bidi. What do you think would have happened if you hadn't been able to put it down that night?

(pauses, reflecting) I don't know... probably I would've lit it, and who knows how long I would've smoked. Maybe I would've gotten caught up in the whole thing again. It's hard to stop when you've been doing it for so long, and it feels like a crutch sometimes. But that time, I guess I was able to be strong enough to put it down.

You're really struggling with the feeling of it being a crutch, and it's hard to break free from that habit after so long. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "feeling like a crutch" and how that affects your desire to quit?

(sighs) It's like, when I'm feeling down or anxious, or just bored like I am now, smoking seems like the easiest thing to do. It's like a habit, a reflex. I don't really think about it, I just do it. And when I'm trying to quit, I feel like I'm fighting that reflex, that habit. It's hard to shake it off, especially when it feels like it's just there, waiting for me to need it again.

It sounds like you feel like smoking is a deeply ingrained habit that's hard to break, and it's especially challenging for you when you're feeling down, anxious, or bored. Can you think of one specific situation, like today, where you felt bored and found yourself reaching for a cigarette, even if it was just in your imagination?

(thinks for a moment) Yeah... today, actually. I was sitting here, feeling a bit restless, and I started thinking about how nice it would be to have a bidi right now. I know I've been trying to cut back, but it felt so tempting just then. But I didn't actually get up and smoke, because I was here in this chair, talking to you.

It sounds like today's situation is a perfect example of a trigger for you, feeling restless and tempted by a bidi. What did you do to distract yourself from the urge to smoke when you were thinking about it just now?

(thinks for a moment) I guess I just sat here and talked to you. It took my mind off of it, a bit. And I was thinking about what you said earlier, about being a healthier father for my daughter. That helped too.

You're saying that we've already started using some strategies that helped distract you from the urge to smoke, and thinking about being a healthier father for your daughter is a powerful motivator. Can you think of other things that you could do in situations like today, where you feel restless and tempted by a bidi, to take your mind off of it and focus on your goals?

(thinks for a moment) Yeah... I could try taking a walk, or reading a book. My daughter likes to send me articles and stories on my phone, so maybe I could read something like that. Or I could try calling her, talking to her about my day. That usually helps me feel a bit better.

You're feeling encouraged about the strategies you've been coming up with to manage cravings, like taking a walk or calling your daughter. What do you think would be the best time of day to put some of these new strategies into practice, since we talked about morning cravings being a challenge for you?

(thinks for a moment) I think mornings would be tough, but maybe right after our sessions like this? You know, when I'm still feeling a bit more engaged, more focused. Maybe if I can make a plan with you to do something after our sessions, like go for a walk or something, I can use that as a reason to stay on track.

You're feeling hopeful about implementing some of these strategies after our sessions. That's a great idea, and I'm happy to support you in making a plan. Can you think of a specific activity you'd like to do immediately after our sessions to help you stay on track with your quitting goals?
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.

It sounds like you're motivated to quit smoking because your son is going through treatment, and you've noticed that you smoke when you feel angry or stressed. Can you tell me more about what exactly happens when you get angry or stressed that makes you reach for a cigarette?

It's just... after a rough day at school, or an argument with my wife about our son... I feel trapped, you know? And then I light a cigarette to calm down, to think clearly. It's like it helps me relax, and then I can deal with the situation better. But I know that's not right.

It sounds like you feel like smoking helps you calm down and clear your head after a stressful or overwhelming situation. Can you think of other activities you've done in the past that helped you relax when you weren't smoking?

I used to like playing guitar, but I haven't picked it up in years. And running, I used to run every morning, but that stopped too. It's hard to think of anything else that really helps me calm down like smoking does.

It sounds like you're struggling to think of other activities that help you relax like playing guitar and running used to. Are those activities you'd be willing to pick up again to help manage your stress and cravings?

I don't know... It's hard to get back into things after such a long time. And I'm not sure if it would help with the cravings when I'm feeling really stressed or angry. Smoking's just so... automatic, you know?

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about picking up old habits again, and worried that they might not be enough to help with your cravings when you're really stressed or angry. Can you imagine trying out just one of those old activities, like playing guitar, for a short period of time - let's say 10-15 minutes - the next time you feel stressed or overwhelmed?
I guess... I could try. But only if it works, okay? I don't want to waste my time if it's not going to help with the cravings. And what if I still want to smoke after 10-15 minutes?

It sounds like you're feeling a bit cautious about trying out old activities to help manage your cravings, and you're worried that they might not work. What do you think would need to happen in those 10-15 minutes of trying out an old activity for you to consider it a success?

I suppose if I can get through that time without feeling like I'm going to lose control and smoke, that would be a success. And if it makes me feel a little bit better, not as angry... that would be good too.

It sounds like you're looking for small, achievable goals to help you feel successful in managing your cravings. What do you think would be a realistic reward for yourself if you can indeed get through those 10-15 minutes without smoking?
I don't know... A decent cup of coffee maybe? Or watching a funny video for a bit? Something to take my mind off things and give me a little boost.

It sounds like you're thinking of small rewards to motivate yourself to try out those old activities. What do you think is the best way to make sure you get your coffee or watch that funny video without getting sidetracked by thoughts of smoking?
Maybe if I tell my wife about my plan, she can remind me to stick to it. Or, I don't know, write it down somewhere so I can see it and remember what I'm trying to do.

It sounds like you're thinking of involving your wife in your plan to help you stay on track, which is a great idea. What specific role do you think she could play in supporting you to manage your cravings and stay smoke-free?

She could be my accountability partner, I guess. Just check in with me and see how I'm doing, remind me of my goals. And maybe she could help me come up with more ideas for things to do when I'm feeling stressed or angry. That would be helpful.

It sounds like you're feeling more confident about having your wife's support, and that's great. What do you think would happen if she checked in with you when you're feeling stressed or angry, and you didn't have any good ideas for coping strategies yet?

I'd have to admit it, I guess. Tell her that I don't know what to do yet. But then she'd have to be patient and help me brainstorm, you know? Not just give me answers, but really help me find something that works for me.

It sounds like you're feeling more open to asking for help from your wife when you're struggling with stress or anger, which is a big step. What do you think would be the hardest part about admitting to her that you're struggling to manage your cravings in those moments?

I don't know... I guess I'd feel a little ashamed, I suppose. Like a failure or something. That I didn't do better, that I couldn't control my cravings. But I guess that's just part of getting better, right?

It sounds like you're feeling a mix of emotions about admitting your struggles to your wife, including shame and fear of failure. Do you think these feelings might be connected to your past experiences of not being able to quit smoking, and if so, how do you think you could use those experiences to your advantage in this new attempt?

Yeah, I think it is. I always used to think that I couldn't quit because I was a failure, because I wasn't strong enough or whatever. But this time... this time I'm thinking that it's not about being strong or weak, it's just about figuring it out, you know? It's not a failure if I slip up, it's just a chance to learn and try again.

It sounds like you're really shifting your mindset about quitting smoking, feeling more hopeful and open to learning from your past experiences. What specific strategies or ideas are you thinking of trying this time around that you weren't trying before?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.
It sounds like you're feeling pretty unsure about being here and might think smoking isn't harming you. What's been making you consider quitting smoking in the first place?

My wife, actually. She's been on my case for years, saying I'm going to get sick and die if I don't stop. But I always just tell her not to worry, that I'll live longer than her anyway. (chuckles) But I guess she finally got me tired of arguing about it, so now I'm here.

You're saying that your wife's pressure has finally worn you down, and that's why you're here to try to quit smoking. Is it fair to say that you're willing to try quitting for her, but you're not entirely sure you're doing it for yourself?

I guess that's fair. I mean, I don't feel like quitting for myself, but if it makes her happy, I'm willing to give it a shot. But if it's too hard, I'm probably going to slip up and go back to smoking. That's just what I do.

You're saying that you're willing to try quitting for your wife, but you're not entirely sure you're committed to it yourself, and you're worried about slipping back into old habits if it gets tough. Can you think of any reasons why you'd want to stay smoke-free, even if it's not just for your wife?

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I guess I've always thought it was just a harmless habit, like drinking a cup of tea. But I suppose I've noticed I'm getting a bit short of breath sometimes, when I'm working in the shop and carrying heavy bags. Maybe being smoke-free would help with that? But it's not like it's a big deal or anything.

You're saying that you've always thought smoking was just a harmless habit, but now you're starting to notice some physical effects like getting tired more easily. Can you think of a situation where you normally smoke, like after a meal or when drinking a cup of coffee, and how you might replace that habit with something else?

Yeah, like after tea in the morning. That's usually when I have a cigarette. But I guess I could replace it with... I don't know, chewing gum or something. That's what my cousin does, I think. Or maybe just taking a walk outside for a minute. That'd be okay, I suppose.

You're saying that you're aware of your morning craving after tea and are considering replacing it with chewing gum or taking a walk. What do you think about trying to do something immediately after you finish your tea, like a specific action that becomes a new habit?

I don't know... it sounds like a bit of a fuss. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to try. Just do something, you know? Like... like going to the back of the shop and straightening up the shelves or something. Yeah, that could work. Not too hard, not too weird.

You're feeling a bit skeptical about trying out a new habit, but you're open to giving it a shot. You've already tried going to the back of the shop and straightening up the shelves, so what do you think about doing something similar right after you finish your tea, like right away, even if it's just for a few seconds?

Yeah, I can do that. It's not like it's a big change or anything. But, it's funny, thinking about it like that... actually doing something, right after tea, makes it sound a bit more... I don't know, purposeful, or something. Maybe that's not a bad idea.

The patient is feeling more open to trying a new habit, especially one that's simple and purposeful, like doing something right after finishing their tea. What do you think about sharing your idea with a family member or friend, so they can support you in making a change?

I'm not sure... I mean, my wife's been on my case about this for years, like I said. But, I suppose if I told her I'm actually trying to quit, she'd feel like she'd won or something. And, you know, it might be good to have someone to tell, I guess. Yeah, maybe I'll mention it to her.

The patient is feeling uncertain about telling their wife about their efforts to quit smoking, fearing she'll feel like she's won an argument. How do you think your wife's reaction might actually be, and could sharing this with her bring you additional motivation to stay on track?

I don't know... I guess she'd probably be happy for me, or relieved or something. And it's true, sharing it with her might help me stay on track, because I'd feel like I'm doing it for myself this time, not just for her. Yeah, maybe that's not such a bad idea after all.

It sounds like you're feeling more open to sharing your efforts to quit smoking with your wife, and that's a great step forward. What do you think would be the best way to have this conversation with her, and how do you think she might respond?

I think I should just be honest with her, tell her what's going on and what I'm trying to do. Not like I'm asking for permission or anything, but just so she knows. And I'm sure she'll be happy and supportive, like you said. I might even tell her about the straightening-up-the-shelves thing, see if she's interested in helping me with that. Yeah, I think that's a good plan.

The patient is saying that they plan to be honest with their wife about their efforts to quit smoking, and they're considering involving her in the process by sharing their strategies, such as straightening up the shelves.

What do you think might happen if your wife suggests additional ways to help you stay on track?

I guess she might have some good ideas, too. I mean, she's always saying she wants to support me, so maybe she's actually been thinking about this stuff. And, you know, it's nice to have someone to share the responsibility with. If she's going to nag me anyway, it's better if she's on my side, right?

The patient is saying they're open to their wife suggesting additional ways to help them stay on track with quitting smoking, and that it's nice to have someone to share the responsibility with. How do you think you'll feel if your wife starts coming up with ideas to help you - will you feel more motivated to follow through with her suggestions?

Yeah, I guess I will. If she's invested in this, it'll be easier for me to stay on track, too. And it's not like I'm doing it for her, exactly... I mean, I am, but it's also like we're working together, you know? Yeah, that's a good feeling.

The patient is saying that having their wife's support and involvement will make it easier for them to stay on track with quitting smoking, and that it feels like they're working together. 

What do you think is the main reason you're feeling more motivated to quit with your wife's support, rather than just trying to quit on your own?
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.
It sounds like you're feeling really discouraged about your attempts to quit smoking in the past. What was one thing that did work a little bit for you in those attempts, even if it didn't last?

I used nicotine gum for a while, it helped to curb the cravings a bit, but I still ended up smoking. I remember thinking it was okay to have a cigarette if I was using the gum, but that only made it worse.

You're saying that using nicotine gum didn't completely stop your cravings for cigarettes, and it even led you to rationalize having a cigarette when you were using the gum. What do you think it is about using nicotine gum that made you think it was okay to have a cigarette afterwards?
I think it was maybe because it made me feel like I was doing something, you know? Like, I was fighting my cravings, but not really. It was like a consolation prize or something.

You're acknowledging that using nicotine gum made you feel like you were taking action against your cravings, but it still didn't stop you from smoking. What do you think would be a more effective way to deal with cravings if you didn't have the nicotine gum?

Honestly, I don't know. I just feel really overwhelmed when I'm thinking about quitting and dealing with cravings at the same time. It's like, I'm trying to quit smoking, but I'm also fighting this monster in my head telling me to just have one more cigarette.

It sounds like you're feeling really overwhelmed by the idea of quitting and dealing with cravings at the same time, like you're fighting an internal battle. Can you think of one thing you do in your daily life that makes you feel in control and less overwhelmed?

Yeah... I guess when I'm at work, I'm pretty focused on my tasks and I feel like I'm on top of things. It's like, I know what needs to be done and I can break it down into smaller tasks and just do them one by one. That helps me feel more in control, I guess.

You're saying that being focused on your tasks and breaking them down into smaller steps helps you feel more in control, which is really helpful to know. Can you think of a way to apply that same sense of focus to dealing with cravings when you feel overwhelmed?

I don't know... I guess it would be good to take it one step at a time, like with work. But it's hard to think about cravings as tasks, you know? It's like, when I'm craving a cigarette, I just want to smoke, not break it down into something I can manage.

You're acknowledging that thinking of cravings as tasks is a challenge for you, and it's hard to break them down when you're in the middle of a craving. Can you think of a moment when you managed to delay acting on a craving for a few minutes, and what helped you do that?

There was one time... I was getting ready for a meeting, and I had a really strong craving to smoke, but I didn't have time to step outside. So I just... just took a few deep breaths and waited until the meeting was over. It wasn't long, but it was something. I think what helped was just knowing that I could put it off for a little while, you know? It didn't make the craving go away, but it... it gave me a tiny bit of control, I guess.

You're saying that even in the midst of a strong craving, you were able to find a way to delay acting on it by taking a few deep breaths and waiting until the meeting was over. Can you think of a way to apply that same strategy to your morning cravings, when you're usually most likely to smoke?

I think I might be able to do something similar, like... taking a short walk around the house or doing a quick stretch to distract myself. But it's hard because, like you said, morning cravings are usually the worst for me. And it's always the same routine, getting ready for work, making breakfast... it's like, I'm so used to having a cigarette right after I eat.

You're acknowledging the challenges of morning cravings and how they fit into your routine. Are you willing to experiment with taking a short walk or doing a quick stretch as soon as you wake up, before starting your morning routine, to see if that helps reduce the intensity of the craving?

I can try it, I guess. It's not like I have anything to lose, right? But it's hard because my brain is already racing with all the things I need to do that morning... I'm not sure if I can actually remember to take a walk or stretch before I start my routine.

The patient is stating that they're willing to try taking a short walk or doing a quick stretch as soon as they wake up, but they're concerned about remembering to do it amidst their busy morning routine. 

Can you think of a way to make it a habit by incorporating it into something you already do every morning, like right after brushing your teeth?

I brush my teeth right after I wake up, so maybe I could just take a few deep breaths or do a quick stretch while I'm standing there, waiting for my toothpaste to spread. It's like a little habit within a habit, you know?

You're saying that you can incorporate taking a few deep breaths or doing a quick stretch into your morning routine by doing it while brushing your teeth. How does it feel to think about making this small change a part of your daily habit?

It's... it's actually kind of hopeful, I guess. I mean, it's not like I'm trying to quit cold turkey or anything, I'm just trying to make a small change. And it's not like it's going to solve everything, but it's something, you know? It's like, maybe I can make it through this morning without smoking, and that's a start, right?

The patient is feeling hopeful about making a small change in their daily routine. Are you feeling a sense of accomplishment thinking about taking action, even if it's just a small step, and does that help you feel more confident about making it through this morning without smoking?

Yeah, it does. I mean, I've been feeling so stuck and powerless over my smoking for a long time, and just thinking about taking that one step - taking a few deep breaths while I brush my teeth - feels like I'm taking back some control. It feels like, maybe I'm not totally powerless after all.

The patient is feeling a sense of control and empowerment by thinking about taking a small step towards quitting smoking. 

What do you think would happen if you were to actually take that step and do the deep breathing while brushing your teeth, rather than just thinking about it?
Look, I work at a dhaba and everyone smokes here. It's part of the culture. I don't really want to quit, but the doctor insisted I come.

It sounds like you're feeling really torn between quitting and feeling like it's a part of your work culture.
